sections.title: Islands of India
sections.content_summary: This section introduces the Andaman and Nicobar Islands as the southernmost tip of India (Indira Point), explaining that an island is land surrounded by water on all sides and that India has over a thousand islands. Students explore the unique forests of the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, home to species found nowhere else such as the Andaman hornbill and Andaman wood pigeon, and learn why these forests need protection. A hands-on activity uses a paper-tube model to understand how buttress roots support trees in the same way a base stabilises a structure. The section also introduces coral reefs at the Marine Interpretation Centre, the North Sentinel Island people's ancient hunter-gatherer lifestyle, and their possible survival of the 2004 tsunami through deep nature-reading — an IKS element about indigenous ecological knowledge.

tasks.task_text: Look at the map for the southernmost point of India.
In which sea are the Andaman and Nicobar Islands located?
Name the other islands you know.

Take a rectangular piece of newspaper or used paper, and roll it into a thin tube. Glue the edges so it does not open. Then, make it stand it up on a flat surface. Does it stay up? Now, cut 3–4 small triangles from thick paper. Stick the triangles evenly around the bottom of the roll. Rest the paper on a flat surface again. Does it stay upright now?
Draw or write about your favourite fish or other sea animal.
List at least three living things found in the sea.
Why do you think life under the sea is just as important as life in the forests?
How do you think the coral reef helps the fish and other sea creatures?
How do you think their knowledge might have helped the people of North Sentinel Island stay safe?

sections.title: Sundarbans: Home of the Mangroves
sections.content_summary: The section describes the Sundarbans as the largest mangrove forest in the world, located where the river Ganga meets the sea on muddy, salty land. Students learn that mangrove roots serve two functions: aerial (breathing) roots help the trees survive in waterlogged salt water, while the root network holds soil and protects coastlines from wave erosion. The section highlights the deep dependence of local communities on mangrove forests across generations — an IKS element of people living in harmony with a fragile ecosystem. It also introduces the tiger-mask tradition used by honey collectors in the Sundarbans as a behavioural adaptation to tiger predation.

tasks.task_text: What do you find in the mangrove forest? List any three.
What do you think makes this place different from other forests?
In what ways do people in Sunderbans depend on the mangrove forests?
Why do you think it is important to take care of places like the Sundarbans?
Look around yourself. Do you see any living thing that has something special to help it live there? What is it?

sections.title: Northeast India: Green Hills and Great Traditions
sections.content_summary: This section introduces Northeast India — the Seven Sisters states plus Sikkim — as a region of lush forests, flowing rivers, and rich biodiversity intertwined with vibrant cultural traditions of weaving, crafts, music, dance, and festivals. Students encounter the community-led Hargila (Greater Adjutant Stork) conservation effort, where women teach children to protect bird nests — a contemporary IKS example of community-nature stewardship. The section also features the living root bridges of Meghalaya, natural structures formed by training tree roots across streams, illustrating how local knowledge harnesses nature's designs. Bhut Jolokia (ghost pepper) is highlighted as a culturally and ecologically significant crop unique to the region.

tasks.task_text: Look at the picture. Identify three things being sold in the market.
Identify the festival from the above picture.
Why do you think the houses are built on bamboo structures?
What animals do you see in the picture?
What does this picture tell you about the people who live with nature in the Northeast of India?
In small groups, choose an animal or bird near you that needs protection. What can you do to help? Create a protection plan and share it with the class.

In small groups, collect twigs, leaves and soft materials like cotton or paper available around you. Create a small bird's nest using the materials. What makes a good nest? What does the nest need to keep the eggs safe?
Try a 'Root bridge challenge' in class. Can you connect two chairs using string or sticks in a way that will hold a small toy?

sections.title: Western Ghats
sections.content_summary: The Western Ghats are introduced as a long chain of forested hills running through six Indian states, recognised as a biodiversity hotspot with many endemic species — including the lion-tailed macaque and the Indian Giant Squirrel — and the source of several major rivers. Students learn about traditional medicine plants found only in this region and the role of tribal and forest-dwelling communities who have depended on the forest for food, medicine, and livelihood across generations — a key IKS element. The section features a citizen-science mango-variety survey led by students, farmers, and experts, and tells the story of the Save Silent Valley Movement as an example of community-led conservation that protected a unique rainforest from a planned dam.

tasks.task_text: Look at the map, locate the Western Ghats and name the six states it passes through.
What animals can you spot in the forest?
What are the people doing in the picture given below?
Why do you think forts were built on hilltops instead of flat land?
Give the names of three rivers that start from the Western Ghats.

Pick a fruit or vegetable that grows in your state. It may have varieties. With the help of your teacher or elders make a list of how many types and varieties of fruits and vegetables are locally known.
List three protected areas in the Western Ghats.

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: This closing section invites students to synthesise their learning from all four regions visited in the chapter — Andaman and Nicobar Islands, Sundarbans, Northeast India, and Western Ghats — by reflecting on personal connections, comparisons with their own region, and what they found most surprising. It includes a matching exercise on aquatic animals and their distinctive features (dolphin, turtle, sea anemone, seahorse, clownfish, starfish, jellyfish, octopus), consolidating marine biodiversity concepts from the Islands section. Students are asked to articulate how people across regions live in harmony with nature and to create a collage depicting vibrant India.

tasks.task_text: What surprised you the most about the places visited in this journey across India? Why?
Which place would you like to visit the most and what would you do there?
How is your region similar or different from one of the places in the story?
Match the following aquatic animals with their features.
What is special about mangroves?
How do people in different regions live in harmony with the nature?
Make a collage by pasting pictures depicting vibrant India.

dual_strand.natural: Unique ecosystems and endemic biodiversity across India's biodiversity hotspots — island forests, mangrove wetlands, northeastern rainforests, and Western Ghats hill ranges, each harbouring species found nowhere else
dual_strand.human_cultural: Human communities living in generational harmony with these ecosystems through indigenous conservation knowledge, community-led wildlife protection, tribal forest dependence, and citizen-led forest conservation movements
